text: The surgeon performs dissection of the left triangular ligament to mobilize the left lateral segment of the liver, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies an accessory left hepatic artery during the procedure, highlighting its presence within the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the gastropathic ligament, revealing the corded lobe beneath.
The surgeon places an external tourniquet around the hepatoduodenal ligament to perform the Pringle's maneuver, temporarily occluding blood flow to the liver.
The surgeon uses an external brachytonicae to retract the hepatic hilum towards the patient's right side, enhancing exposure of the caudate process and Spiegel lobe.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound, displaying the tumor limits on the screen to ensure precise identification within the liver tissue.
The surgeon begins the transection on the medial side of the Spiegel lobe, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the liver tissue.
The surgeon identifies small branches of the left portal vein during the hepatectomy procedure, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the section with blunt detachment of the vena cava in the posterior plane of the Spiegel lobe, following the initial medial approach.
The surgeon sections the ligament to the diaphragmatic cross, effectively separating the Spiegel lobe from the vena cava.
The surgeon alternates between medial and posterior approaches to advance the dissection of the Spiegel lobe, carefully navigating around the identified tumor limits.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, located cranially to the left accessory hepatic artery, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon achieves improved mobilization of the spigel lobe following the dissection and separation of small branches from the left portal vein.
The surgeon completes the liver transection, visibly separating the liver tissue along the planned transection line.
The surgeon removes the liver specimen by enlarging the incision along the left border, facilitating its extraction from the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a revision of hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding, and applies a fibrinogen and thrombin sealant to the surgical site for enhanced hemostatic control.
The final view shows the port used for the external tourniquet and the enlarged port used for specimen removal.
